summary: When I was about five years old, I lived in Burma during a very difficult time. The Burmese soldiers restricted our Karen people from going outside, and we struggled with food shortages. My family had only one cow, but the soldiers wouldn't let us feed it. My mom risked her life to feed the cow, and we lived in constant fear of the soldiers. Eventually, in 2006, my family decided to leave Burma because my dad was very afraid and didn’t want to stay there anymore.

After leaving Burma in 2006, my family moved to a refugee camp in Thailand. Life there was hard, but we received free rice and some other food. My parents worked every day to support us; my father would sneak out and climb over mountains to find jobs with local Thai people, even though it was illegal. My mother stayed home weaving traditional Karen clothes to earn a little money. Despite the difficulties, we were grateful to be alive and safe in Thailand.

In 2012, the American government opened its doors for refugees like us to immigrate, and we applied for resettlement. Our family was accepted, and we arrived in the United States on September 29, 2013. At first, we didn’t know anyone and stayed inside crying every day, but over time I met more Karen people and felt less alone. I am very grateful to live in America where I have opportunities to learn and attend a great school. It has been a blessing for my family to live happily here.

When my family arrived in Minnesota in 2013, we didn’t know anyone and it was very hard at first. We stayed inside our house and cried every day because everything was so new and unfamiliar. But after almost a year and a half, I got to know more Karen people in Minnesota and I stopped feeling so sad. Minnesota has become our new home, and I am thankful for the opportunities to learn and live happily here with my family.
cues: almost a year and a half
